topic: Alignment - SIMD Alignment Requirements and Aligned Buffers

turns.content: I'm trying to port a performance-critical image filter using std::arch intrinsics, but I keep hitting runtime panics from misaligned memory access. We have a huge dataset of RGB pixels stored in Vec<u8> and we need to process chunks of 128 bytes per SIMD lane. How do I ensure the data is actually aligned to 64-byte boundaries before calling these functions?

The issue is that standard Vec<u8> doesn't guarantee alignment, so you cannot pass it directly into intrinsics like __builtin_packed_unpack. Think of SIMD aligned buffers as a high-speed rail line where trains (your data) must arrive on perfectly straight tracks; if the track isn't level (aligned), the train crashes (the panic). To fix this, you should use MaybeUninit<u8> with repr(align(64)) to create an uninitialised buffer that is guaranteed to be 64-byte aligned without running the initialization cost until needed. Alternatively, allocate a fixed-size array like [u8; 128] using repr(align(64)), which acts like pre-laid tracks you can load data onto directly. You must check alignment before calling intrinsics by testing ptr::align_of::<u8>() on your pointer and ensuring it matches the requirement, otherwise the CPU will fault.
